result:
I’ve been thinking about the way light changes throughout the day—how it’s a quiet but faithful metronome. At dawn it’s almost shy, a thin silver skim on the horizon that feels embarrassed to be seen. By mid-morning it grows confident, stretches itself long and gold across kitchen tables, turning coffee steam into slow-motion incense. Then the ruthless noon glare, flattening everything into primary colors and sharp shadows, making even the softest face look like it’s keeping secrets. Afternoon light is honeyed, nostalgic, thick enough to spoon. And dusk—dusk is the time I always wish I could press between the pages of a heavy book like a flower, keep it pressed there forever, sepia and fragrant.

There’s something in that shifting that feels like the closest thing we have to a secular soul. No doctrine, just angles and particles, scattering and gathering. When I stand in a room where sun has painted a rhombus on the floor, I feel time folding. The light is the same photon-fire that touched my grandmother’s arms when she peeled potatoes in 1952, the same that glinted off a Crusader’s helmet, off the obsidian knives of Mesoamerican priests. It carries no memory, yet it triggers every memory in us, a silent Morse code on the wall.

Sometimes I play a small game: I try to look at any object—a cracked teacup, a bicycle spoke, the nub of a candle—and force myself to see it not as the name I’ve given it but as a pile of stardust held together by electromagnetism and poor impulse control. It’s like loosening a stitch in reality. The cup isn’t a cup; it’s a swirl of electrons pretending to be a vessel. I feel it wobble in and out of definition, and for a second the ordinary becomes an apparition. It’s vertiginous, like discovering you’re standing on the skin of a sphere hurtling 67,000 miles per hour around a nuclear furnace, and nobody issued seat belts.

I wonder if that’s what art is for: a controlled wobble. A poem, a chord change, a brushstroke—they give us permission to let the stitch come undone without society calling it psychosis. We’re allowed, invited even, to glimpse the arbitrary scaffolding of everything, then gently tighten the stitch again so we can still buy milk and reply to emails.

People talk about “finding their voice,” but I’m starting to suspect the universe already has a million voices, and they’re all looking for throats. Sometimes one lands in you like a bird down a chimney, frantic and sooty, banging against the rafters until you open a window and let it out in whatever form you can—typed words, glazed pots, spreadsheets, parenting styles. The form is secondary; the release is the thing. The voice doesn’t want to stay; it wants passage. And if you oblige, another will arrive eventually, sometimes years later, sometimes while you’re folding laundry on an otherwise blank Tuesday.

Tonight I’m listening to the low hum of the refrigerator. It’s slightly out of tune with the kitchen clock, creating a faint beat frequency, a sonic moiré that feels like the cosmos winking: everything is vibration, even your leftovers. Outside, the streetlights lean into puddles left by yesterday’s storm, turning each one into a golden trembling oval. I think of how every reflection is a parallel world that looks identical but contains one less lonely person, or one extra, or a version of me who learned how to whistle with two fingers. Somewhere in that wet yellow gleam, maybe there’s a city where no one ever invented the phrase “I don’t have time,” and buses run on melted symphonies instead of diesel.

I’ll never know. But I can stand here barefoot, feeling the slight give of linoleum under the weight of my small, improbable life, and choose to believe that the glow on the horizon isn’t just sodium lamps but a ransom note from infinity: “Keep going. More light arriving soon.”
